title: The healthy child whose urine tests 'positive for sugar'
blurb: An incidental, completely benign finding — a reducing substance in the urine that is fructose, not glucose, from a harmless enzyme quirk.

patient.complaint: Routine urine dipstick flagged 'reducing substances'; otherwise perfectly well.

opening: 5-year-old boy, thriving and asymptomatic, whose pre-op urine screen showed a positive Clinitest (reducing substances) but a NEGATIVE glucose-oxidase dipstick. He has no symptoms — no hypoglycemia, no vomiting, normal growth, normal liver. The lab confirms the urinary reducing substance is fructose, and it rises after he eats fruit. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Entirely well, normal development and growth, no episodes of vomiting or hypoglycemia, eats fruit freely without ill effect. Found incidentally. Family: a parent recalls being told the same thing as a child (autosomal recessive, benign).
exam: Completely normal. No hepatomegaly, no jaundice, normal growth parameters, normal neuro exam.

prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why is this BENIGN (no hypoglycemia or liver injury) unlike hereditary fructose intolerance?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the first step of fructose metabolism that is deficient here.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — fructokinase (ketohexokinase). Without it, fructose is simply not phosphorylated to fructose-1-phosphate; it accumulates in blood and spills harmlessly into urine. Because NO fructose-1-phosphate is trapped, there is no phosphate sequestration, no ATP depletion, no hypoglycemia, and no liver injury — the disorder is benign.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The benign block is the very FIRST step — phosphorylating fructose. (Aldolase B, one step later, is the dangerous HFI defect.)
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: Fructokinase (Fructose → Fructose-1-P). No F1P forms, so the disorder is asymptomatic — the perfect contrast to HFI.

mcqs.stem: Why is essential fructosuria benign while HFI is dangerous?
choices: Fructokinase deficiency prevents F1P formation entirely (nothing toxic accumulates); HFI lets F1P form but not be cleaved, trapping phosphate and depleting ATP
mcqs.explanation: The toxin in HFI is trapped fructose-1-phosphate. In fructokinase deficiency, F1P is never made — fructose just circulates and is excreted. Same sugar, one enzyme apart, opposite clinical consequences.

mcqs.stem: Why does the Clinitest read positive while the glucose dipstick reads negative?
choices: Clinitest detects any reducing sugar (including fructose); the dipstick uses glucose oxidase and is specific for glucose
mcqs.explanation: Clinitest (copper reduction) reacts with any reducing substance — fructose, galactose, lactose. The glucose-oxidase dipstick is glucose-specific. A positive Clinitest with a negative dipstick points to a non-glucose reducing sugar in the urine.

mcqs.stem: What management does essential fructosuria require?
choices: Reassurance — no treatment or dietary restriction is needed
mcqs.explanation: It is a harmless incidental finding; the main 'treatment' is recognizing it so the child isn't mislabeled diabetic or subjected to needless restriction. Contrast with HFI, which demands strict lifelong avoidance of fructose/sucrose/sorbitol.
